headline: CATTLE SURGE OVERNIGHT, GRAINS DRIFT LOWER
subheadline: Live cattle jumped 1.8% on stronger beef demand signals while corn and wheat lost ground ahead of spring planting decisions.
lead: Livestock markets caught fire overnight with cattle futures up nearly 2% on renewed beef demand strength, while grains drifted lower in lackluster trade. The dollar's weakness couldn't spark export interest in corn or beans, suggesting domestic fundamentals are driving this market. With March planting intentions looming, producers face critical acreage decisions in a sideways price environment.
teaser: Cattle rally sparks feed cost questions while grain markets search for direction.

one_number.context: This 1.8% overnight surge represents the strongest cattle move in weeks, signaling renewed beef demand confidence. For corn producers, higher cattle prices mean stronger feed demand potential, but the $4.44 corn price isn't reflecting that optimism yet. The disconnect suggests either cattle are getting ahead of themselves or grain markets are missing the story.

sections.title: GRAINS & OILSEEDS
sections.body: Corn lost ground despite stronger livestock prices, closing at $4.44 after dropping 2½¢ in quiet trade. December corn at $4.70 shows the market's not panicking about new-crop supplies, but it's not excited either. Soybeans barely budged at $11.66, with meal down 1.4% while oil gained 1.1% — another sign of crushing demand struggling against protein meal weakness. Wheat dropped 7¢ to $5.68 as Black Sea competition continues pressuring U.S. export prospects.
sections.bottom_line: Grains treading water while livestock markets run hot.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current pricing positions — no compelling reason to act on today's modest moves.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK & DAIRY
sections.body: Cattle markets exploded higher with live cattle up 1.8% to $238.52 and feeders surging 2.1% to $360.85 on stronger beef demand indicators. This rally suggests tighter cattle supplies are finally getting market recognition, which should theoretically boost feed demand for corn and meal. Hogs joined the party with a 1.4% gain to $97.12, while Class III milk added 50¢ to $17.62. The livestock strength contrasts sharply with weak grain performance, creating a feed cost advantage for producers who forward-priced protein.
sections.bottom_line: Strongest livestock rally in weeks signals tighter supplies meeting demand.
sections.farmer_action: Livestock producers should consider protecting these higher prices — cattle rallies this sharp often correct quickly.

sections.title: ENERGY & INPUTS
sections.body: Crude oil gained 70¢ to $76.11 while natural gas collapsed 3.1% to $2.93, creating a mixed input cost picture ahead of spring fieldwork. Lower natgas prices should translate to cheaper nitrogen fertilizer costs within 4-6 weeks, but diesel costs remain elevated with crude above $76. The energy divergence reflects heating demand destruction meeting persistent transportation fuel needs. For farmers planning spring applications, the natgas weakness is the first good news in months.
sections.bottom_line: Natgas collapse hints at cheaper fertilizer while diesel stays expensive.
sections.farmer_action: Delay additional nitrogen purchases if possible — prices should improve through March.

sections.title: MACRO & TRADE
sections.body: The dollar index dropped 0.4% to $98.78, typically bullish for ag exports, but grains couldn't capitalize on the currency weakness. This suggests domestic supply concerns are outweighing export optimism as March planting intentions approach. The S&P 500 gained 0.8% to $6,869, maintaining risk-on sentiment, while Bitcoin's 6.6% surge to $72,815 signals continued speculative appetite. The macro backdrop should favor commodities, but agricultural markets are clearly focused on seasonal fundamentals over financial flows.
sections.bottom_line: Dollar weakness fails to spark grain exports ahead of planting decisions.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor USDA's March 31 Prospective Plantings report — it will matter more than currency moves.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Livestock Rallies Often Stall
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 1.8% cattle surge looks impressive, but protein markets have a history of sharp rallies that quickly reverse. Unlike grains, livestock futures represent a perishable product with limited storage flexibility. When demand signals strengthen, prices can gap higher overnight — but they're equally prone to fast corrections if that demand doesn't materialize at retail. The key is watching beef cutout values and export sales data in the coming days to see if this rally has legs.

watch_list.desc: Weekly Export Sales — will grain exports respond to dollar weakness?
Cattle cutout values — need to confirm demand behind overnight surge
USDA Crop Progress — first fieldwork reports from the South
Prospective Plantings report — the season's most critical acreage data

daily_quote.text: You can't make up in September what you lost in June.

source_summary: USDA · CME Group · Reuters · Market Intelligence
